items.title: Risk factors and BRCA
items.contentHtml:
Risk factors and BRCA

  • BRCA1/2: germline mutation; cumulative risk of breast cancer up to 72% (BRCA1) and 69% (BRCA2) at age 80.
  • BRCA1 is associated with triple negative; BRCA2 to positive hormone receptors.

items.title: BI-RADS Classification
items.contentHtml:
BI-RADS classification in mammography

  • BI-RADS 0: incomplete evaluation, requires additional images.
  • BI-RADS 1: negative; BI-RADS 2: benign findings.
  • BI-RADS 3: probably benign (<2% malignancy); 6-month follow-up.
  • BI-RADS 4: suspicious, subcategories 4A (low suspicion 2-10%), 4B (intermediate 10-50%), 4C (high 50-95%); requires biopsy.
  • BI-RADS 5: highly suggestive of malignancy (≥95%); immediate biopsy.
  • BI-RADS 6: known malignancy confirmed by biopsy.

items.title: hereditary breast cancer
items.contentHtml:
Hereditary breast cancer

  • Suspected criteria: breast cancer <50 years old, bilateral, triple negative, ovarian cancer, male, multiple affected relatives.
  • Main genes: BRCA1, BRCA2, TP53 (Li-Fraumeni syndrome), PTEN (Cowden), CDH1 (hereditary diffuse gastric cancer).
  • Management: bilateral risk-reducing mastectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (BRCA1/2) around 35-40 years of age.
  • Chemoprevention with tamoxifen reduces risk by 50% in BRCA2 carriers (not in BRCA1).

items.title: Histological types
items.contentHtml:
Most frequent histological types

  • Infiltrating ductal carcinoma (80%): the most frequent; intermediate prognosis.
  • Infiltrating lobular carcinoma (10-15%): loss of E-cadherin, diffuse growth, difficult mammographic detection, bilaterality.
  • Medullary carcinoma: well delimited, infiltrating lymphocytes, better prognosis.
  • Mucinous carcinoma: abundant extracellular mucus, good prognosis, typical in elderly.
  • Tubular carcinoma: low grade, excellent prognosis.
  • Papillary carcinoma: intracystic, good prognosis.

items.foco: Management of ductal carcinoma in situ
items.title: Management of DCIS
items.contentHtml:
Management of ductal carcinoma in situ

  • Treatment: conservative surgery (lumpectomy with free edges ≥2 mm) + adjuvant radiotherapy; alternative total mastectomy if multifocal or positive margins.
  • In low-grade DCIS and <2.5 cm, radiotherapy can be omitted (ECOG-ACRIN E5194).
  • Sentinel lymph node: indicated if mastectomy or if invasion is highly suspected.
  • Adjuvant tamoxifen reduces ipsilateral and contralateral recurrence in RE+ DCIS (NSABP B-24).
  • Not known use chemotherapy or trastuzumab.

items.title: Hormonal receptors and HER2
items.contentHtml:
Hormone receptors and HER2

  • Estrogen receptor (ER) and progesterone receptor (PR): positive if ≥1% of cells stained; favorable prognosis, guide endocrine therapy.
  • HER2: overexpression (IHC 3+) or gene amplification (FISH ratio ≥2.0); poor prognosis without anti-HER2 therapy.
  • Triple negative (ER-, RP-, HER2-): 15-20%, greater aggressiveness, basic chemotherapy, no benefit from endocrine or anti-HER2.
  • Luminal A subtype (ER+, HER2-, low proliferation) better prognosis; luminal B (ER+, HER2+ or high proliferation) worse.

items.title: TNM staging
items.contentHtml:
TNM staging of breast cancer

  • T: T1 ≤2 cm, T2 >2-5 cm, T3 >5 cm, T4 extension to the chest wall or skin (T4a: wall, T4b: edema/ulceration, T4c: both, T4d: inflammatory carcinoma).
  • N: N0 without lymph node metastases; N1: mobile ipsilateral axillary nodes; N2: fixed or conglomerates; N3: infraclavicular or supraclavicular.
  • M: M0 without distant metastases; M1 metastasis.
  • Stages: 0 (DCIS), I (T1N0M0), II (T0-3N1M0 or T2-3N0M0), III (any T N2-3M0 or T4 any N M0), IV (M1).
  • Inflammatory carcinoma always T4d and stage IIIB at least.

items.title: Tamoxifen and adverse effects
items.contentHtml:
Tamoxifen and adverse effects

  • Tamoxifen: selective estrogen receptor modulator (SERM); dose 20 mg/day for 5-10 years.
  • Adverse effects: hot flashes, vaginal dryness, increased risk of venous thromboembolism (VTE) and endometrial cancer (relative risk 2-3).
  • Contraindicated in women with a history of VTE or endometrial hyperplasia.
  • In premenopausal women: blocks estrogen receptor; in postmenopausal women: partial agonist in endometrium and bone.
  • Alternative in postmenopause: aromatase inhibitors (anastrozole, letrozole, exemestane) without endometrial or VTE risk.

items.title: Surgery: quadrantectomy vs mastectomy
items.contentHtml:
Surgical treatment: quadrantectomy vs mastectomy

  • Quadrantectomy (breast-conserving surgery): removal of the tumor with a margin of healthy tissue; equivalent in survival to total mastectomy for tumors ≤4 cm and adequate breast (NSABP B-06).
  • Conservation contraindications: multicentric disease, extensive microcalcifications, previous radiotherapy, pregnancy (first trimester), positive margin after re-excision.
  • Total mastectomy: removal of the entire mammary gland; indicated in multifocality, recurrence after conservation, or patient preference.
  • Modified radical mastectomy (Patey): includes axillary dissection levels I-II.

items.foco: Sentinel lymph node in breast cancer
items.title: sentinel lymph node
items.contentHtml:
Sentinel lymph node in breast cancer

  • Technique: peritumoral/subareolar injection of technetium-99m + methylene blue; identification of the first lymphatic drainage node.
  • Indicated in cN0 invasive breast cancer (clinically negative).
  • If sentinel node positive: complete axillary dissection if >2 positive sentinel nodes or macrometastasis (ACOSOG Z0011: omit dissection if T1-T2, 1-2 positive sentinel nodes, conservative surgery + radiotherapy).
  • False negatives: 5-10%; greater if multifocal tumor or previous surgery.

items.title: Fibrocystic disease
items.contentHtml:
Fibrocystic breast disease

  • Benign changes: fibrosis, cysts, epithelial hyperplasia; more common in women 30-50 years old.
  • Histological classification: non-proliferative (no increased risk), proliferative without atypia (relative risk 1.5-2), proliferative with atypia (relative risk 4-5).
  • Mammography: round/ovoid cysts, benign microcalcifications (bubble type).
  • Management: symptomatic (avoid caffeine, NSAIDs); aspiration of painful cysts; biopsy if suspected.
  • No special follow-up required except atypia (monitoring every 6-12 months).

items.title: Galactorrhea and prolactinoma
items.contentHtml:
Galactorrhea and prolactinoma

  • Galactorrhea: non-puerperal milk secretion; causes: prolactinoma, drugs (antipsychotics, metoclopramide), hypothyroidism, chronic stimulation.
  • Diagnosis: prolactin levels >200 ng/mL suggest prolactinoma; between 20-200 ng/mL rule out other causes.
  • Image: MRI of the sella turcica with gadolinium to detect microadenoma (<10 mm) or macroadenoma (≥10 mm).
  • First-line treatment: cabergoline 0.5-2 mg/week (dopamine agonist); reduces prolactin and tumor size.
  • Transsphenoidal surgery if resistance/intolerance or chiasmatic compression.
